metadata.title: Differences in Circulating Gluconeogenic Substrates During Short-Term Fasting in Men, Women, and Children
metadata.study_design: Comparative experimental study with fasting protocol
metadata.demographics: 20 adults (10 men and 10 women) and 15 prepubertal children (6.1 ± 0.8 yr)

evidence.quote: Morey W. Haymond, Irene E. Karl, William L. Clarke, Anthony S. Pagliara, and Julio V. Santiago
20 adults (10 men and 10 women) were fasted for 86 hr and 15 children (6.1 _+ 0.8 yr, mean + SE) were fasted for 30 hr
Metabolism, Vol. 31, No. 1 (January), 1982
Ten normal healthy adult male and ten female subjects and 15 prepubertal children were admitted to the Clinical Research Unit
20 adults (10 men and 10 women) were fasted for 86 hr
15 children (6.1 _+ 0.8 yr, mean + SE) were fasted for 30 hr
20 adults (10 men and 10 women) were fasted for 86 hr
15 children (6.1 _+ 0.8 yr, mean + SE) were fasted for 30 hr
all had fasting glucose concentrations < 110 mg/dl

methods_participants.label: Inclusion Criteria - Carbohydrate Intake
methods_participants.value: at least 200 gm carbohydrate per day for three days prior

evidence.quote: The adults were consuming at least 200 gm carbohydrate per day for three days prior to the study
all had fasting glucose concentrations < 110 mg/dl and were consuming a weight-maintaining diet prior to study

methods_participants.label: Exclusion Criteria - Diabetes History
methods_participants.value: None of the subjects had a family history of diabetes

evidence.quote: None of the subjects had a family history of diabetes

methods_participants.label: Exclusion Criteria - Hypoglycemia
methods_participants.value: None had history compatible with hypoglycemic episodes or documented hypoglycemia

evidence.quote: None of the subjects had a history compatible with hypoglycemic episodes or documented hypoglycemia

methods_participants.label: Blood Sampling - Initial Times
methods_participants.value: 1800-2000 hr, 0800 hr, then at 4 hr intervals

evidence.quote: Bloods were obtained between 1800-2000 hr, 0800 hr, and subsequently at 4 hr intervals

methods_participants.label: Blood Collection Method
methods_participants.value: 4-5 milliliter of blood drawn into syringe containing Na heparin

evidence.quote: Four to five milliliter of blood were drawn into a syringe containing Na heparin (0.1 ml)

methods_participants.label: Assay Method - Glucose, Amino Acids, Ketones

evidence.quote: Plasma glucose, alanine, glutamine and glutamate and blood lactate, pyruvate, beta-bydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate were determined microfluorometrically
Amino acid determinations were performed on Spinco-Beckman 119 Amino Acid Analyzer using standard physiologic methodology
Plasma glucagon, insulin, and growth hormone concentrations were determined by radioimmunoassays
Plasma glucagon, insulin, and growth hormone concentrations were determined by radioimmunoassays
Plasma glucagon, insulin, and growth hormone concentrations were determined by radioimmunoassays
cortisol by a protein binding assay

methods_participants.label: Amino Acid Sampling - Men and Women
methods_participants.value: all sampling times in six men and six women

evidence.quote: Plasma amino acid concentrations were determined at all sampling times in six men and six women

methods_participants.label: Amino Acid Sampling - Children
methods_participants.value: 0, 14, 22, and 30 hr fasted samples of five children

evidence.quote: on the 0, 14, 22, and 30 hr fasted samples of five children

narrative_results.statement: Glucose concentrations were similar in the three groups studied during the first 18 hr of fasting

evidence.quote: Glucose concentrations were similar in the three groups studied during the first 18 hr of fasting (Fig. 1).

narrative_results.statement: By 30 hr fasting, the plasma glucose concentration was lowest in children and highest in the men

evidence.quote: By 30 hr fasting, the plasma glucose concentration was lowest in children and highest in the men and the groups were statistically different one from another

narrative_results.statement: Beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate concentrations rose more rapidly and to higher values in the children than in the adults

evidence.quote: Beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate concentrations rose more rapidly and to higher values in the children (3.75 _+ 0.35 mM and 1.26 _+ 0.12 mM, respectively) than in the adults (0.91 _ 0.15 mM and 0.38 +_ 0.06 mM, respectively in the men and 1.68 _+ 0.20 mM and 0.62 _+ 0.06 mM in the women) over the initial 30 hr fasting (p < 0.001).

narrative_results.statement: Beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations were higher in women than in men during the entire period of study

evidence.quote: During the entire period of study, beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations were higher in women than in men (p < 0.001)

narrative_results.statement: An inverse relationship was observed between plasma glucose and beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations in each group

evidence.quote: An inverse relationship (p < 0.001) was observed between plasma glucose and beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations in each group (r = 0.934, 0.925, 0.961, men, women and children, respectively).

narrative_results.statement: Blood lactate concentrations were higher in children than in adults between 6 and 30 hr of fasting

evidence.quote: Between 6 and 30 hr of fasting, blood lactate concentrations were higher in children than in adults (p < 0.002).

narrative_results.statement: Plasma alanine concentrations were lower in women than men over the entire study

evidence.quote: over the entire study, plasma alanine values were lower in women than men.
Fig. 1.
Fig. 2.
Fig. 4.
Fig. 5.

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes inclusion criteria about diet; Pass 2 describes amino acid sampling methodology. These are unrelated facts from different sections
Pass 1 describes amino acid sampling in men and women, while Pass 2 describes sampling in children. These are different study populations and sampling protocols.

semantic_groups.rationale: Pass 1 includes redundant unit label 'years' at end; both convey identical age data for 15 children
Identical extraction of fasting duration for adults
Identical extraction of fasting duration for children
Identical inclusion criteria for fasting glucose threshold
Same carbohydrate intake requirement; Pass 2 provides broader context about diet type but both capture the 200g carbohydrate criterion
Pass 1 describes inclusion criteria about diet; Pass 2 describes amino acid sampling methodology. These are unrelated facts from different sections
Both convey no family history of diabetes; Pass 1 includes 'None of the subjects had' phrasing while Pass 2 is more concise
Both exclude subjects with hypoglycemia history; Pass 1 slightly more detailed but conveys same exclusion criterion
Both describe identical blood sampling times: initial samples at 1800-2000 hr and 0800 hr, followed by 4-hour intervals. Only minor wording differences.
Pass 1 contains blood collection method details; Pass 2 is empty. Only one side present.
Both describe microfluorometric assay method for glucose, amino acids, and ketones. 'Microfluorometrically' and 'Microfluorometric determination' are equivalent expressions.
Both identify Spinco-Beckman 119 Amino Acid Analyzer. Capitalization difference in 'Amino Acid Analyzer' vs 'amino acid analyzer' is negligible.
Both specify radioimmunoassay for glucagon. Pass 2 uses plural 'radioimmunoassays' while Pass 1 uses singular, but this is a minor variation.
Both specify radioimmunoassay for insulin. Pass 2 uses plural 'radioimmunoassays' while Pass 1 uses singular, but this is a minor variation.
Both specify radioimmunoassay for growth hormone. Pass 2 uses plural 'radioimmunoassays' while Pass 1 uses singular, but this is a minor variation.
Both specify protein binding assay for cortisol. Capitalization difference is negligible.
Pass 1 describes amino acid sampling in men and women, while Pass 2 describes sampling in children. These are different study populations and sampling protocols.
Pass 1 contains information about amino acid sampling in children, but Pass 2 is empty, making this an orphan entry.
Both passages describe identical study population information: 20 adults with equal gender distribution.
Identical text across both extractions
Identical text across both extractions
Identical text across both extractions
Identical text across both extractions
Identical text across both extractions
Same fact with different word order: 'were lower in women than men over the entire study' vs 'Over the entire study, plasma alanine values were lower in women than men'
Identical text across both extractions
Pass 1 has no extraction while Pass 2 contains a valid fact about alanine concentration changes in children vs adults
